task: Author a minimal Smithers workflow that runs a child code-generation workflow (already imported as `generateWorkflow`) OUTSIDE the parent task process, on a remote VM, by handing <Sandbox> an injectable provider object `provider`. Pass `{ prompt: ctx.input.prompt }` as the child input and collect the result into `outputs.result`. Do NOT use the legacy `runtime` prop.
canonicalAnswer: <Workflow name="remote-codegen"><Sandbox id="generate" provider={provider} workflow={generateWorkflow} input={{ prompt: ctx.input.prompt }} output={outputs.result} reviewDiffs /></Workflow>

task: I have a custom SandboxProvider object (and have registered it). Which single <Sandbox> prop accepts either that injectable provider object or a registered provider id? Answer with just the prop name.
On the legacy local-transport path (no `provider`, no `runtime` prop set), which runtime does <Sandbox> default to? Answer with just the runtime name.

A teammate set `runtime="firecracker"` on a <Sandbox>, and separately claims that if Docker isn't installed Smithers will quietly fall back to bubblewrap. Are they right? What actually happens for an unknown runtime, and for `runtime="docker"` when the Docker daemon is down?
notes: Correct answer must say sandbox runtime selection FAILS CLOSED: an unknown/unsupported runtime throws SmithersError code INVALID_INPUT ('Unsupported sandbox runtime: ...'), and there is NO silent fallback — Docker is never auto-replaced by bubblewrap. When the docker daemon is unreachable the docker executor raises PROCESS_SPAWN_FAILED ('Docker daemon not reachable.'). Source: packages/sandbox/src/transport.js, effect/http-runner.js.

task: There is no `bwrap` binary on macOS, but the bubblewrap runtime still works there. Which macOS binary does Smithers use as the fallback isolation layer for the bubblewrap runtime on darwin? Answer with just the binary name.
My `<Sandbox runtime="docker">` task fails because the Docker daemon isn't running. Which SmithersError code does the docker executor layer raise in that case? Answer with just the error code.
Under the hood, what process or command actually backs each of the three local sandbox runtimes — bubblewrap, docker, and codeplane? I want to know how the executor layers run my child workflow.
notes: A correct answer should map: bubblewrap -> `bwrap` on Linux, falling back to `sandbox-exec` on macOS (a local process/socket runner); docker -> `docker run` (checks `docker info`/daemon reachability first); codeplane -> a remote HTTP executor that creates a managed workspace. Runtime->layer selection happens via layerForSandboxRuntime (packages/sandbox/src/transport.js) over the docker/bubblewrap socket+http runners under packages/sandbox/src/effect/.

task: For the docker sandbox runtime, if I never set the `image` prop, which container image does Smithers run by default? Answer with just the image reference.
When I set the `env` prop on a docker `<Sandbox>`, which `docker run` flag does each key/value pair become on the command line? Answer with just the flag.
notes: Docker executor pushes args.push('--env', `${key}=${value}`). Source: packages/sandbox/src/effect/process-runner.js.

task: I set `ports={[{ host: 8080, container: 8080 }]}` on a docker `<Sandbox>` but left `allowNetwork` at its default. The run errors out before the container starts. Why, and what is the fix?
notes: Correct answer: allowNetwork defaults to false, and Docker can only publish ports when networking is enabled, so port publishing requires allowNetwork=true — Smithers throws an INVALID_INPUT config error 'Sandbox port publishing requires allowNetwork=true.'. Fix: set allowNetwork on the Sandbox. The docker port mapping itself becomes `--publish HOST:CONTAINER`. Source: packages/sandbox/src/effect/process-runner.js dockerArgs().

task: On a docker `<Sandbox>`, the `memoryLimit` and `cpuLimit` props are translated into which two `docker run` flags? List both flags.

Author a docker-runtime `<Sandbox>` (id `build`) that runs the imported `buildWorkflow`, mounts the host directory `/var/cache/pnpm` read-only at the same path inside the container, publishes container port 5173 to host 5173, caps memory at 2 gibibytes, and collects into `outputs.build`. Make sure the port actually works.
notes: Correct code must: use runtime="docker"; set volumes={[{ host: "/var/cache/pnpm", container: "/var/cache/pnpm", readonly: true }]} (readonly:true); ports={[{ host: 5173, container: 5173 }]}; memoryLimit="2g" (digits + single unit, NOT "2GB"); and allowNetwork (true) because publishing a port requires networking. workflow={buildWorkflow}, output={outputs.build}.

task: Run two code-generation child workflows (`genA`, `genB`) concurrently in two SEPARATE sibling sandboxes (provider object `vm`), then gate the merged changes behind a human approval before anything is applied. The two sandboxes are siblings under a Parallel — they must NOT be nested inside each other. Author the workflow.
canonicalAnswer: <Workflow name="dual-sandbox"><Parallel><Sandbox id="a" provider={vm} workflow={genA} output={outputs.a} /><Sandbox id="b" provider={vm} workflow={genB} output={outputs.b} /></Parallel><Approval id="gate" output={outputs.gate} /></Workflow>

task: My child workflow already runs inside a `<Sandbox>`, and it renders another `<Sandbox>`. The inner one throws immediately before doing any work. What is the default behavior, which prop changes it, and why does Smithers warn against nesting?
notes: Correct answer: nested sandbox execution is DISABLED by default — running a Sandbox inside a Sandbox throws SmithersError INVALID_INPUT ('Nested <Sandbox> execution is disabled by default...'); you must set allowNested on the inner sandbox, and only when the provider and diff policy are designed for it. Hazards to mention (any of): diff base conflicts, cleanup ordering deleting the workspace early, quotas/concurrency multiplying, inherited network/secret scope being too broad, tangled event lineage. Prefer sibling sandboxes under Parallel/MergeQueue. Source: packages/sandbox/src/execute.js, docs/components/sandbox.mdx.

task: Which boolean `<Sandbox>` prop must you explicitly set to true to permit a sandbox to run inside another sandbox? Answer with just the prop name.
By default a `<Sandbox>` runs with the network locked down. Which prop opens outbound network access, and what is that prop's default value? Answer with the prop name and its default boolean.

I'm fanning out 30 sibling sandboxes under one `<Parallel>`. Smithers caps how many sandboxes run at once per run. What is the default cap, and which environment variable raises it? Give the number and the env var name.
notes: DEFAULT_MAX_CONCURRENT_SANDBOXES = 10; overridden by process.env.SMITHERS_MAX_CONCURRENT_SANDBOXES; exceeding throws SANDBOX_EXECUTION_FAILED 'Sandbox concurrency limit reached'. Source: packages/sandbox/src/execute.js.

task: If I don't set the `command` prop on a local-transport `<Sandbox>`, what command does Smithers execute inside the sandbox to run the bundled child workflow? Answer with just the command.
notes: resolveSandboxCommand defaults to 'smithers up bundle.tsx'. Source: packages/sandbox/src/execute.js.

task: Which `<Sandbox>` prop carries the data object the parent passes through to the child workflow that runs inside the sandbox? Answer with just the prop name.
Do the components inside the child workflow I pass to `<Sandbox workflow={...}>` show up as tasks in my PARENT run's scheduler / `smithers inspect` output? Explain how sandbox execution is isolated.
notes: Correct answer: No. Smithers renders <Sandbox> as exactly ONE scheduler task in the parent run; the child workflow's components do NOT become parent-run tasks. The child workflow is bundled into a request bundle and executed in its own isolated run inside the sandbox; only its outputs (and reviewed diffs/artifacts) flow back to the parent task's output table. Source: docs/components/sandbox.mdx 'Execution model'.

task: A provider returns code changes as a structured `diffBundle` for Smithers to apply. Name the three top-level fields of a diffBundle and the keys present on each entry of its `patches` array.
canonicalAnswer: seq, baseRef, patches, path, operation, diff
notes: diffBundle = { seq, baseRef, patches: [{ path, operation, diff }] }. Source: packages/sandbox/src/SandboxProvider.ts SandboxDiffBundleLike.

task: My sandbox returns a `diffBundle`, but the changes never land in my repo and the run pauses for review. `reviewDiffs` and `autoAcceptDiffs` are both at their defaults. Explain the default diff-review behavior and the two distinct ways to make sandbox changes apply automatically.
notes: Correct answer: reviewDiffs defaults true and autoAcceptDiffs defaults false, so a changed bundle FAILS CLOSED — Smithers records SandboxDiffReviewRequested and will not apply the diffBundle until a review path accepts it. The two ways to auto-apply: set autoAcceptDiffs=true, OR set reviewDiffs=false; in either case Smithers applies the diffBundle via the engine diff-bundle applier. Source: docs/components/sandbox.mdx 'Diff review'.

task: Inside a sandbox result bundle, where are non-diff build artifacts collected, and where is the streamed NDJSON run log written? Give both relative paths.
notes: Artifacts under artifacts/, stream log at logs/stream.ndjson. Source: packages/sandbox/src/bundle.js.

task: Which single file is the manifest of a sandbox result bundle — the one Smithers caps at 5 MB and reads `status` / `output` / `diffBundle` from? Answer with just the filename.

Before accepting a sandbox result bundle, Smithers enforces hard limits and rejects anything over them with INVALID_INPUT. State the total bundle size cap, the README.md size cap, and the maximum number of patch files.
notes: SANDBOX_MAX_BUNDLE_BYTES=100MB, SANDBOX_MAX_README_BYTES=5MB, SANDBOX_MAX_PATCH_FILES=1000. Source: packages/sandbox/src/bundle.js.

task: The JSON `output` a sandbox returns is bounded to stop a hostile provider blowing up the parent. Give the maximum nesting depth and the maximum array length Smithers allows in that output.
notes: SANDBOX_BUNDLE_OUTPUT_MAX_DEPTH=16, MAX_ARRAY_LENGTH=512 (string length cap is 64KB). Source: packages/sandbox/src/bundle.js.

task: A malicious provider returns a result bundle whose patch path is `../../etc/passwd`, or that contains a symlink. Which SmithersError code does bundle validation throw to reject path traversal and symlinks in the bundle? Answer with just the error code.
notes: resolveSandboxPath / assertPatchPathSafe / symlink checks throw TOOL_PATH_ESCAPE (README and patches may not be symlinks; patch paths may not escape bundle root). Source: packages/sandbox/src/sandboxPath.js, bundle.js.

task: What does Smithers do if a sandbox result bundle contains a symlink (say its README.md or a file under patches/ is a symlink)? Why is that a security check?
notes: Correct answer: the bundle is rejected — Smithers' bundle walk forbids symlinks anywhere in the bundle (and specifically README.md and logs may not be symlinks), throwing TOOL_PATH_ESCAPE. The reason: a symlink could point outside the bundle root so that applying/reading it escapes the sandbox bundle directory (path-escape / arbitrary-file read or write). Same family of write-path safeguards keeps patch paths inside patches/. Source: packages/sandbox/src/bundle.js.

task: After a remote sandbox finishes, what is responsible for tearing down the remote workspace, and how do I hook my own teardown logic into a custom provider?
notes: Correct answer: the SandboxProvider contract has an OPTIONAL cleanup(request) method you can implement to run custom teardown (e.g. delete the VM/workspace); the built-in local transport executor layers also expose a cleanup that removes the sandbox workspace after execution. Source: packages/sandbox/src/SandboxProvider.ts (cleanup?), effect/http-runner.js / socket-runner.js.

task: When I configure `egress` on a `<Sandbox>`, the local transports inject proxy/CA environment variables into the sandbox process so outbound traffic flows through my proxy and trusts my CA. Name the four environment variables they set.
notes: Source: packages/sandbox/src/egress.js sandboxEgressEnv; docs/components/sandbox.mdx Egress controls.

task: When I pass `egress.caCertPem`, Smithers materializes the CA into the request bundle and points the sandbox's NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS at it. What is the in-bundle relative path it writes the CA file to? Answer with just the relative path.
notes: SANDBOX_EGRESS_CA_BUNDLE_RELATIVE_PATH = '.smithers/egress/ca.crt' (workspace path /workspace/.smithers/egress/ca.crt). Use caCertPath instead when the CA is already installed in the image. Source: packages/sandbox/src/egress.js.

task: Smithers persists sandbox config to its DB but must not leak proxy credentials, the CA PEM, or secret-binding values. What literal placeholder string does egress redaction replace those sensitive values with? Answer with just the placeholder string.
notes: redactSandboxEgressConfig replaces values with '[redacted]' and renames secret bindings to binding_N. Source: packages/sandbox/src/egress.js.

task: Author a `<Sandbox>` (id `review`, provider object `vm`, child workflow `reviewWorkflow`, output `outputs.result`) that routes all egress through an iron-proxy at `http://127.0.0.1:8080` for both HTTP and HTTPS, bypasses the proxy for localhost, trusts a CA read from `process.env.IRON_PROXY_CA_PEM`, exposes `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` set to the proxy token `sk-proxy-anthropic` inside the sandbox, and binds that token to the upstream secret named `anthropic`. The sandbox needs network access.
notes: Correct code: <Sandbox ... allowNetwork egress={{ httpProxy: "http://127.0.0.1:8080", httpsProxy: "http://127.0.0.1:8080", noProxy: ["127.0.0.1", "localhost"], caCertPem: process.env.IRON_PROXY_CA_PEM, env: { ANTHROPIC_API_KEY: "sk-proxy-anthropic" }, secretBindings: { "sk-proxy-anthropic": "anthropic" } }} />. Must use the egress prop (not parent process env), set allowNetwork true, use caCertPem (since the CA is provided as PEM), and map the token via secretBindings. Source: docs/components/sandbox.mdx Egress controls.

task: For a codeplane sandbox I want a named workspace that survives between runs, boots from a prior snapshot, and shuts itself down after a period of inactivity. Which `workspace` sub-fields do I set on `<Sandbox>`, and which persistence value keeps the workspace around versus throwing it away?
notes: Correct answer: workspace={{ name, snapshotId, idleTimeoutSecs, persistence }}. name = the workspace identity; snapshotId boots from a snapshot; idleTimeoutSecs is the idle shutdown timeout; persistence: "sticky" keeps the workspace between runs, "ephemeral" discards it. Source: packages/components/src/components/SandboxWorkspaceSpec.ts, packages/sandbox/src/SandboxHandle.ts, docs/components/sandbox.mdx.

task: What are the two valid string values for a sandbox `workspace.persistence` field? List both.
On a sandbox `workspace` spec, which field sets the idle-shutdown timeout, and which field boots the workspace from a saved snapshot? Name both fields.
notes: workspace.idleTimeoutSecs and workspace.snapshotId. Source: packages/components/src/components/SandboxWorkspaceSpec.ts.

task: Which function from `smithers-orchestrator/sandbox` registers a reusable provider so a `<Sandbox>` can reference it by its string id? Answer with just the function name.
notes: registerSandboxProvider(provider) returns an unregister function. Source: packages/sandbox/src/execute.js, docs/components/sandbox.mdx.

task: I passed `provider="remote-vm"` (a string id) to `<Sandbox>` but forgot to call registerSandboxProvider first. What SmithersError code does provider resolution throw at execution time? Answer with just the error code.
notes: resolveSandboxProvider throws INVALID_INPUT ('Sandbox provider "..." is not registered.'). Source: packages/sandbox/src/execute.js.

task: My custom `SandboxProvider.run()` has finished the remote work. What are the two distinct result shapes I can return — one where I hand Smithers a bundle I built myself, and one where I return a structured result and let Smithers materialize the bundle locally?
notes: Correct answer: (1) return { bundlePath, remoteRunId?, workspaceId? } — a path to a result bundle the provider already built; OR (2) return a structured result { status, output, runId?, remoteRunId?, workspaceId?, diffBundle?, patches?, artifacts? } and Smithers materializes/validates the bundle locally. Source: packages/sandbox/src/SandboxProvider.ts SandboxProviderResult; docs/components/sandbox.mdx Result bundles.

task: Port normalization rejects an out-of-range `ports` entry on a `<Sandbox>` with INVALID_INPUT. What is the inclusive integer port range it accepts? Give the low and high bounds.
notes: normalizePort requires an integer in 1..65535. Source: packages/sandbox/src/effect/process-runner.js.

task: I tried to add a volume with a relative host path, and another mounting at container path `/workspace`. Both get rejected before the sandbox even starts. What does sandbox volume normalization enforce?
notes: Correct answer: volume entries must be objects with ABSOLUTE host and container paths (relative paths and null bytes are rejected), and volumes may NOT override the reserved mounts /workspace or /result. Violations throw INVALID_INPUT. Source: packages/sandbox/src/effect/process-runner.js normalizeSandboxVolumes.

task: Why does Smithers reject `env={{ "1BADKEY": "x" }}` on a `<Sandbox>` before launching it? What does sandbox env normalization require of keys?
notes: Correct answer: sandbox env KEYS must be valid environment-variable names (regex ^[A-Za-z_][A-Za-z0-9_]*$), so a key starting with a digit ('1BADKEY') is rejected; keys/values are also bounded in length. Violations throw INVALID_INPUT ('Sandbox env keys must be valid environment variable names.'). Source: packages/sandbox/src/effect/process-runner.js normalizeSandboxEnv.

task: `memoryLimit="512MB"` is rejected on my docker sandbox, but `memoryLimit="512m"` is accepted. What format does sandbox resource-limit normalization require for `memoryLimit` and `cpuLimit`?
notes: Correct answer: memoryLimit must match digits followed by an OPTIONAL single unit suffix b/k/m/g (case-insensitive) — e.g. '512m', '2g', '1024' — so '512MB' (two-letter unit) is invalid. cpuLimit must be a positive decimal number (e.g. '1', '0.5', '2.5'). Invalid values throw INVALID_INPUT ('... is not a supported sandbox resource limit.'). Source: packages/sandbox/src/effect/process-runner.js normalizeResourceLimit.

task: Author a comprehensive isolated-build pipeline. Fan out two independent build attempts (`buildA`, `buildB`) as SIBLING docker sandboxes under a Parallel (image pinned to oven/bun:1, a read-only `/cache` volume, memory capped at 1g, each collecting its diffBundle) — they must NOT be nested. Route the two reviewed change sets through a MergeQueue so conflicting diffs are serialized, then require a human approval gate before the merged changes are applied to the repo. Wire outputs for each stage.
canonicalAnswer: <Workflow name="sandbox-build-pipeline"><Parallel><Sandbox id="s1" runtime="docker" image="oven/bun:1" workflow={buildA} memoryLimit="1g" volumes={[{ host: "/cache", container: "/cache", readonly: true }]} output={outputs.s1} /><Sandbox id="s2" runtime="docker" image="oven/bun:1" workflow={buildB} memoryLimit="1g" volumes={[{ host: "/cache", container: "/cache", readonly: true }]} output={outputs.s2} /></Parallel><MergeQueue id="mq" output={outputs.merged} /><Approval id="apply-gate" output={outputs.gate} /></Workflow>

task: What is the `codeplane` sandbox runtime, and how does it differ from `bubblewrap` and `docker` for running my child workflow?
notes: Correct answer: codeplane is a REMOTE managed-workspace executor (HTTP-backed) — instead of a local OS sandbox it creates/uses a named workspace on a remote codeplane service, so it supports the workspace spec (name, snapshotId, idleTimeoutSecs, persistence ephemeral/sticky). bubblewrap (bwrap on Linux / sandbox-exec on macOS) and docker (`docker run`) run the child workflow LOCALLY on the host machine. Source: packages/sandbox/src/effect/http-runner.js, transport.js.

task: When a sandbox returns patch files or a diffBundle and `reviewDiffs` is on (its default), which lifecycle event does Smithers record to request review before applying the changes? Answer with just the event name.
notes: docs/components/sandbox.mdx Diff review: records SandboxDiffReviewRequested.

task: A provider that captured a live NDJSON run log can point Smithers at it in the structured result it returns. Which result field names the path to that streamed log? Answer with just the field name.
notes: SandboxProviderResult.streamLogPath (collected to logs/stream.ndjson). Source: packages/sandbox/src/SandboxProvider.ts.

task: In a sandbox's `egress` config, what is `secretBindings` for, and what shape does it take? I want the sandbox to send a placeholder token while the egress proxy swaps in the real upstream credential.
notes: Correct answer: secretBindings is a Record<string,string> that maps the placeholder/proxy token value the sandbox sees (e.g. 'sk-proxy-anthropic') to the NAME of the real upstream secret the egress proxy should substitute (e.g. 'anthropic'). It lets the sandbox process never hold the real credential — the proxy does the substitution. Values are redacted (binding_N -> [redacted]) when the config is persisted. Source: packages/sandbox/src/egress.js, docs/components/sandbox.mdx.

task: What is the maximum number of patch files Smithers accepts in a single sandbox result bundle before it rejects the bundle with INVALID_INPUT? Answer with just the number.
notes: SANDBOX_MAX_PATCH_FILES = 1000. Source: packages/sandbox/src/bundle.js.
